title: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval
exclusions: Does NOT verify the root cause of the emergency; focuses on the governance loop only.
evidence: Saved search 'breakglass_post_use_review' runs every 30 min; writes per-incident review status to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AC-6(7) requires review of privileges; break-glass use demands post-hoc approval by design.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AC-6 (Least privilege) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.5.15 access control — emergency access procedures require review and sign-off.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.15 (Access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Privileged access must be timely reviewed — break-glass is the exception that most frequently fails an ITGC review.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.AccessMgmt.Privileged (Privileged access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a break-glass account is used but no matching approval record exists 48 hours after session close, the UC fires naming the account, session_id, and initiator.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Usages with an approval record and sign-off by the designated reviewer do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=pam breakglass=true earliest=-14d
| stats min(_time) as started max(_time) as ended values(session_id) as session_id by account initiator
| join account session_id [search index=grc sourcetype=breakglass:approval | stats values(approver) as approver max(_time) as approved_at by account session_id]
| where isnull(approved_at) AND (now()-ended)>172800
| table account initiator started ended session_id approved_at
description: Every break-glass activation must be followed by a reviewer sign-off within 48 hours. The UC surfaces any usage that escaped the governance loop.
value: Break-glass misuse is the most audited sub-case of privileged access; a missing approval is a textbook SOX-ITGC deficiency.
implementation: (1) Tag break-glass accounts at vault time; (2) emit approval events from the GRC tool; (3) schedule every 30 min; (4) on miss, notify CISO and engage SOAR. Indexes required: `index=grc`, `index=pam`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=breakglass:approval`.
visualization: Table of break-glass activations with approval status, bar chart of usages per quarter, single-value 'activations with 48h approval'. Key fields: `account`, `approved_at`, `approver`, `ended`, `initiator`, `session_id`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `pam`, `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AC-6`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 AC-6 (Least privilege) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `pam`, `grc` under `breakglass:approval`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tag break-glass accounts at vault time. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** emit approval events from the GRC tool. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** schedule every 30 min. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on miss, notify CISO and engage SOAR. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIST 800-53 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates `index=pam` (sourcetype=`breakglass:approval`) on the outer side with `index=grc` (sourcetype=`another`) on the inner side, joined on `account session_id` across the last 14 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `account session_id` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use app]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.40.2,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=pam sourcetype=breakglass:approval earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `account`, `approved_at`, `approver`, `ended`, `initiator`, `session_id`, `session_id]`, `started`. Run:

```spl
index=pam sourcetype=breakglass:approval earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table account, approved_at, approver, ended, initiator, session_id, session_id], started
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.40.2: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Break-Glass Activations With Approval Status (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Usages Per Quarter (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Activations With 48H Approval' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show who used powerful accounts and when, so access reviews and investigations have a clear story.
